Epilepsy — the Virtual Epileptic Patient: The story opens in 2017 with Jirsa, who built a personalized Virtual Epileptic Patient brain model in a single bitemporal epilepsy patient by integrating structural connectivity, the epileptogenic zone, and MRI lesions, then fit and validated it against that patient's SEEG data. In the same year, Proix 2017 scaled the idea outward, using diffusion-MRI-derived patient-specific virtual brain models to simulate seizure propagation across fifteen drug-resistant patients while accounting for propagation patterns and postsurgical success. Proix 2018 then turned to the underlying dynamics, introducing a neural field model whose two mechanisms — excitable-media wave propagation and coupled-oscillator dynamics across multiple time scales — explained the diversity of seizure propagation and termination, again confirmed in patient data. The focus next shifted toward intervention and excitability: An 2019 combined modularity analysis with patient-specific simulation in The Virtual Brain to find effective, safe surgical targets outside the epileptogenic zone, while Courtiol 2020 used an extended Epileptor to compare epileptic and healthy brains and found interictal epileptic brains had lower global excitability but locally hyperexcitable epileptogenic regions. A methodological thread then emerged around inference, with Vattikonda 2021 using Bayesian inference to optimize the Epileptor into patient-specific propagation models validated on synthetic data and twenty-five patients, Makhalova 2022 applying virtual epileptic patient modeling retrospectively across fifty-three patients to detect epileptogenic regions, and Wang 2023 carrying that VEP-plus-Bayesian-inference workflow to fifty-three drug-resistant focal epilepsy patients to estimate epileptogenic zone networks. Alongside this, Dollomaja 2023 extended the whole-brain Epileptor approach to a new clinical question, modeling status epilepticus from one patient and showing propagation depends on connectome and network state, while Azilinon 2024 enriched the same VEP pipeline by adding quantitative sodium-23 MRI features as priors to improve epileptogenic zone estimation. Cai 2024 stands apart by using a coupled Wendling neural mass model rather than the Epileptor, fitting SEEG from twelve patients to simulate virtual radiofrequency thermocoagulation and predict surgical outcomes, before Dollomaja 2025 closes the arc by generating and openly releasing a cohort of thirty virtual epilepsy patients with ground-truth epileptogenic-zone parameters validated against empirical intracranial recordings.
Alzheimer's disease & dementia: We start back in 2018, where two quite different approaches appear side by side. Penny and colleagues fitted dynamic causal models, using a Jansen-Rit formulation, to EEG from presymptomatic PSEN1 carriers, finding a hierarchical inter-hemispheric model optimal and showing that connectivity differences predicted later MMSE trajectories; in the same year Zimmermann used The Virtual Brain with a Reduced Wong-Wang model across 124 individuals spanning healthy aging, MCI, and Alzheimer's, where model parameters correlated with cognition and even exceeded the predictive capacity of empirical connectomes. From there the TVB line branches by mechanism: Stefanovski in 2019 built personalized virtual brains with the Jansen-Rit model in which PET-derived amyloid-beta modulated excitation-inhibition balance to reproduce EEG slowing and its potential reversal by memantine, while Arbabyazd in 2021 took the Wong-Wang route to infer virtual FC from SC and back, showing classifiers on virtual connectomes matched empirical performance. The Wong-Wang thread then deepens with Monteverdi, whose 2022 study found subject-specific excitation/inhibition parameters that differed between neurodegenerative phenotypes and improved discrimination when combined with neuropsychological scores, and whose 2023 follow-up extended this directly to contrast Alzheimer's against frontotemporal dementia, revealing disease-specific connectivity and synaptic differences tied to clinical and pharmacological profiles; alongside it, Triebkorn in 2022 combined empirical PET/MRI with TVB-simulated local field potentials to push machine-learning classification of Alzheimer's roughly ten percent higher. We also see the question itself widen: Martinez-Cañada in 2023 used 1/f slopes from resting EEG/MEG with a spiking LIF network to infer regional excitation/inhibition abnormalities, and Patow that same year used a dynamic mean-field model to disentangle amyloid-beta versus tau, finding amyloid dominated early MCI stages while tau dominated later AD. Finally the Jansen-Rit, EEG-based digital-twin idea matures with Amato, whose 2024 personalized cortical model fitted from individual resting EEG outperformed standard analysis in separating MCI from healthy subjects and flagging CSF AD hallmarks, and whose 2025 DADD digital-twin successor scaled this to 124 participants, identifying CSF-positive patients with 88 percent accuracy and predicting clinical conversions with 87 percent accuracy.
Schizophrenia: We can trace the modeling of schizophrenia across three studies that tackle the same pathology from quite different angles. We start in 2015 with Yang, who used a large-scale connectivity model with the reduced Wong-Wang equations and resting-state fMRI from 161 patients, finding that an elevation in excitation-inhibition balance predicted hyperconnectivity, most prominent in association cortices and correlating with symptoms. Adams in 2022 keeps the excitation-inhibition theme front and centre but changes both the model and the data: rather than a large-scale connectivity model on fMRI alone, this study uses dynamic causal modeling of a canonical microcircuit applied to EEG and fMRI paradigms across patients, relatives, and controls, and it explains the characteristic EEG changes through greater pyramidal-cell self-inhibition, with disinhibition predicting psychotic symptoms. Notice how the question itself shifts here, from where hyperconnectivity emerges to which specific cell-level mechanism best accounts for the recorded signals. Finally, Mana in 2023 takes yet another approach, swapping in a Hopf or Stuart-Landau oscillator model and analyzing resting-state fMRI from 47 patients and 118 controls through whole-brain model perturbations, revealing increased globally coherent states and pinpointing temporo-parietal and medial fronto-temporal areas that drive the pathological dynamics. So across these three, the same disease is interrogated with different neural mass models, different modalities, and a moving question, from circuit excitation-inhibition, to cell-level self-inhibition, to the spatial drivers of pathological whole-brain dynamics.
Parkinson's disease: Let's walk through these Parkinson's studies from oldest to newest, because together they show how differently you can attack the same pathology. We start in 2020 with Maith, who fit a spiking neuro-computational basal ganglia model to BOLD correlations between basal ganglia nuclei, building subject-specific Parkinson and control models and recovering firing-rate and connectivity differences consistent with prior experiments. Two years later, Jung 2022 zooms out to the whole brain, using a Jansen-Rit dynamical model to generate simulated BOLD and then combining those simulated and empirical connectome features with behavioural model fitting to improve machine-learning classification of patients versus healthy controls, so here the model is a means to a diagnostic end rather than a description of nuclei. Also in 2022, Meier takes yet another route, co-simulating a detailed spiking basal ganglia model with The Virtual Brain's whole-brain mean-field model and showing that virtual deep brain stimulation moved a patient's thalamus-basal ganglia firing rates toward healthy levels while altering distributed cortical activity, effectively marrying the fine-grained basal ganglia detail of the Maith line with a whole-brain context. Finally, Angiolelli 2025 moves to a clinical, multimodal setting, analyzing EEG and subthalamic deep electrodes in ten Parkinsonian patients before and after L-Dopa and using a novel dopamine-dependent neural-mass model to correctly infer higher ON-state and lower OFF-state dopaminergic tone. So across these four we see the focus shift from BOLD-fitted basal ganglia nuclei, to whole-brain simulation for classification, to co-simulated virtual deep brain stimulation, and finally to a dopamine-aware model grounded in EEG and intracranial recordings, each making a different choice of model, data, and question.
Stroke: Let's walk through these two stroke studies in order. The earlier one, Falcon 2015, used The Virtual Brain with the Stefánescu-Jirsa 3D model to simulate BOLD signals for twenty stroke patients and ten controls, and the headline finding there was that the long-range coupling parameter correlated negatively with global efficiency, but not with centrality. The second, Falcon 2016, is a clear successor from the same group, again using The Virtual Brain and the same Stefánescu-Jirsa 3D model, but this time modeling fMRI in a comparable cohort of twenty patients alongside eleven controls. What's interesting is how the question shifts: where 2015 was concerned with how coupling relates to network efficiency and centrality, 2016 broadens the focus to a richer set of model parameters, reporting reduced conduction velocity, increased local dynamics, and reduced local inhibitory coupling in stroke. And crucially, 2016 ties those parameter changes to motor recovery, moving from a purely network-topology framing toward a more mechanistic, clinically relevant account of how the same modeling pipeline can illuminate the trajectory of recovery after stroke.
Brain tumor / glioma: We start in 2018 with Aerts and colleagues, who used The Virtual Brain to simulate brain dynamics in 25 brain tumor patients and 11 controls, showing that individually optimized Reduced Wong-Wang parameters improved the prediction of functional connectivity and helped differentiate the tumor-affected regions. That first study really established the question of whether personalized model fitting can capture how a tumor reshapes individual brain dynamics. The 2020 study by Aerts builds directly on this line of work, keeping the same Reduced Wong-Wang model in The Virtual Brain but shifting the question from baseline characterization toward the surgical trajectory. Here the focus is on stability and intervention: the authors found that the brain network model parameters were stable across tumor resection, and then went a step further by performing virtual neurosurgery on the resected connectomes. Notably, that virtual neurosurgery improved post-surgical functional connectivity similarity in only some patients, which tempers the earlier optimism with a more nuanced, patient-specific result. So across these two studies we see a consistent modeling framework applied to the same pathology, but moving from individualized parameter optimization for diagnosis-style differentiation toward a longitudinal, treatment-oriented use of the model around surgery.
Multiple sclerosis: For multiple sclerosis we have a single study on the slide, Martí-Juan from 2023, which uses the Reduced Wong-Wang model in The Virtual Brain. What makes this one stand out is its scale and its clinical reach: it brings together 513 multiple sclerosis patients alongside 208 healthy controls, drawn from seven different centers, which is a substantial multi-site cohort for this kind of modeling work. The central question here is not just about the disease itself but about cognition, specifically how the brain's wiring relates to cognitive impairment. The key result is that higher coupling between structural and functional connectivity was associated with cognitive impairment, indexed by low scores on the Symbol Digit Modalities Test. Because this is the only study shown here, there is no earlier work on the tile for it to build upon, so I won't draw any lineage; instead, take it as a self-contained example of how structure-function coupling derived from a virtual brain model can be linked directly to a clinically meaningful cognitive outcome.
Traumatic brain injury: For traumatic brain injury, the slide rests on a single study, Good and colleagues from 2022, so let me take a moment with it. Here the team turned to The Virtual Brain and a dynamic mean-field model, fitting it to whole-brain resting-state fMRI in a cohort of 44 TBI patients. What is striking about the approach is that it does not stop at describing the imaging data, but uses the model to recover hidden parameters of brain dynamics that you cannot read off a scan directly. In particular, they found that lower regional inhibitory connection strengths were associated with more symptoms. Crucially, those same inhibitory parameters also predicted worse cognitive outcomes at six months, giving the model a genuinely prognostic role. So even as a standalone example, it shows the throughline of this whole section, taking resting-state fMRI and a personalized whole-brain model and extracting clinically meaningful, individual-level markers from it.
Disorders of consciousness: For disorders of consciousness, the slide rests on a single study, Luppi from 2022, so rather than tracing a progression across competing approaches we can dwell on how this one work frames the problem. Luppi and colleagues take a dynamic mean-field approach, but what makes their setup distinctive is the way they ground a perturbed whole-brain haemodynamic model in real biological measurements, combining PET maps of GABA with diffusion MRI of structural connectivity. By perturbing that model, they could ask not just whether consciousness is lost but how it is lost. Their answer is that increased local inhibition and randomised connectivity represent two distinct routes to unconsciousness, one characterising anaesthesia and the other disorders of consciousness. It is a nice illustration of how a single mechanistic model, when fed the right modalities, can separate superficially similar clinical states into mechanistically different paths.
Depression: Both studies on this slide tackle depression with The Virtual Brain's Jansen-Rit model, yet they approach the pathology from quite different angles. The earlier work, An 2022, builds high-resolution personalized Virtual Big Brain models that reconstruct over 100,000 individually stimulable fiber tracts, and uses them to simulate deep brain stimulation in treatment-resistant depression; its key observation is that the resulting event-related potentials depend on where you stimulate, though the authors are careful to note validation limitations. Hofsähs 2026 keeps the same Jansen-Rit modeling foundation but shifts both the modality and the question, simulating TMS-evoked potentials rather than DBS-driven event-related potentials. Instead of asking where to stimulate, it asks what underlying circuit deficit drives the pathology, showing that introducing GABAergic deficits raised TEP amplitude toward MDD-like pathological levels. That mechanistic framing lets it point toward specific inhibitory therapeutic targets, so the two together trace a move from stimulation-site mapping in the personalized structural model toward identifying the inhibitory mechanism behind the depressive signature.
Obsessive-compulsive disorder: For obsessive-compulsive disorder we have a single study to consider, Naze and colleagues from 2025. What makes their approach distinctive is that they don't rely on any one source of evidence; instead they bring together neuroimaging, behavioural data, and computational modelling, using a Reduced Wong-Wang model to tie those strands together. The question they pursue is fundamentally a mechanistic one about coupling within frontostriatal circuits. Concretely, they found that decreasing ventromedial frontostriatal coupling while at the same time increasing dorsolateral cortico-striatal coupling delivered the highest functional improvements in obsessive-compulsive disorder. So the headline here is a directional, circuit-specific intervention: turning one pathway down and another up, rather than a uniform change across the network. Because this is the only study on the slide, there is no earlier work here for it to build upon, but it nicely illustrates how a multimodal, model-based strategy can point toward a targeted treatment hypothesis.
